name: Problem Translation Challenge
description: Learners receive problems stated in different ways — technical language, everyday language, pictures, and stories — and must restate each problem in their own words. Partners then compare restatements to see if they captured the same essential question. Develops the habit of understanding before solving and reveals how different framings highlight different aspects of the same problem.
materials: Problem cards (15-20 problems at varying difficulty)
learning_objectives: Restate problems accurately in own words
Identify the essential question beneath surface details
Recognize that different restatements can highlight different aspects
Develop the habit of understanding before attempting solutions
variations: Cross-language: restate a math problem as a story, or a story problem as a diagram
Telephone version: restatements pass through three people, check if the core question survives
Challenge mode: restate a problem in exactly 10 words

name: What Do We Know / What Do We Need? Sorting Activity
description: Given a problem scenario with multiple pieces of information, learners sort information cards into three categories: Known (information we have), Unknown (information we need to find), and Irrelevant (information that does not help). Develops the critical skill of identifying what matters and what is distraction — essential for tackling complex, real-world problems where not all information is relevant.
materials: Sorting mat with three labeled columns
learning_objectives: Identify known information in a problem
Identify what information is needed to solve the problem
Recognize and set aside irrelevant information
Articulate why certain information is or is not relevant
variations: Missing information: some problems deliberately lack a key piece — learners must identify what is missing
Overload version: add many irrelevant details to test filtering skills
Create-your-own: learners write problems with hidden irrelevant information for classmates

name: Strategy Toolkit Rotation
description: Groups of learners receive the same problem, but each group is assigned a different strategy (work backwards, draw a diagram, make a table, find a pattern, simplify first). Each group solves the problem using only their assigned strategy, then presents their approach to the class. The class compares strategies: Which was easiest? Which revealed the most insight? Which was most efficient? Demonstrates that multiple valid approaches exist and that strategy choice matters.
materials: Problem handout (same problem for all groups)
Large paper or whiteboard for each group
learning_objectives: Apply a specific problem-solving strategy deliberately
Explain how a strategy leads to a solution
Compare the effectiveness of different strategies on the same problem
Recognize that multiple valid approaches exist
variations: Speed round: which strategy reaches the answer fastest?
Difficulty scaling: repeat with a harder version of the same problem structure
Free choice round: after seeing all strategies, choose the best one for a new problem and justify

name: Work Backwards Mystery Puzzle
description: Learners receive puzzles where the end state is given and they must figure out the starting conditions. Examples: a number goes through a sequence of operations and ends at 42 — what was the starting number? A maze has an exit marked but no entrance — trace backwards to find the start. A recipe produces a certain dish — given the dish, reconstruct the ingredients. Builds fluency with the work-backwards heuristic through engaging, concrete challenges.
materials: Work-backwards puzzle sheets (8-10 puzzles at varying difficulty)
learning_objectives: Apply the work-backwards strategy to reverse a sequence of steps
Recognize problem types where working backwards is more efficient than working forwards
Verify solutions by checking them in the forward direction
Explain when and why working backwards is a useful strategy
variations: Create-your-own: design a work-backwards puzzle for a classmate
Real-world version: plan a morning routine backwards from 'arrive at school on time'
Multi-step: puzzles with 5+ steps that require careful tracking

name: SCAMPER an Everyday Object
description: Learners select a common everyday object (pencil, chair, backpack, water bottle) and systematically apply all seven SCAMPER prompts: Substitute (what could replace a part?), Combine (what could be merged with it?), Adapt (what else is this like?), Modify (what could be bigger, smaller, or different?), Put to other use (what else could it do?), Eliminate (what could be removed?), Reverse (what if it worked the opposite way?). Each prompt generates 2-3 ideas. Develops creative thinking through structured divergent thinking.
materials: Idea recording sheets with space for each prompt
learning_objectives: Apply all seven SCAMPER prompts systematically to generate ideas
Generate multiple creative variations on an existing design
Distinguish between divergent thinking (generating ideas) and convergent thinking (evaluating ideas)
Select the most promising ideas from a larger set using criteria
variations: Group SCAMPER: each group member takes one prompt, then combine all ideas
Competitive SCAMPER: which team generates the most unique ideas in 10 minutes?
Real innovation: research an actual product redesign that used one of the SCAMPER principles

name: Analogical Problem Solving
description: Learners solve a problem in one domain (e.g., how ants find the shortest path to food), study the solution structure, then apply the same structural solution to a problem in a completely different domain (e.g., how to route network traffic efficiently). The key insight is recognizing that problems with different surface features can share the same deep structure. Builds the transfer skill that is the ultimate goal of problem-solving education.
materials: Paired problem sets (source domain and target domain)
learning_objectives: Identify the structural solution in a source problem
Map structural elements from one domain to another
Apply a solution strategy from a familiar domain to an unfamiliar one
Articulate what makes two problems structurally similar despite surface differences
variations: Reverse analogy: given the target problem, find a source analogy from a different domain
Historical analogies: study real innovations inspired by biological or natural analogies
Multiple targets: apply the same source solution to three different target domains

name: Escape Room Team Challenge
description: A multi-puzzle challenge requiring different problem-solving skills: spatial reasoning (physical puzzle), logical deduction (code-breaking), pattern recognition (sequence completion), language play (word puzzle), and mathematical reasoning (calculation challenge). No single person has all skills, so teams must identify each member's strengths, distribute tasks accordingly, and integrate findings. Includes a metacognitive debrief where teams discuss what worked and what they would change.
materials: Escape room puzzle kit (5-6 interconnected puzzles)
learning_objectives: Identify and leverage different team members' strengths
Distribute problem-solving tasks effectively within a group
Communicate findings clearly to teammates under time pressure
Reflect on group dynamics and collaboration effectiveness
variations: Create-your-own: teams design escape rooms for other teams
Remote version: solve puzzles collaboratively via shared digital workspace
Themed versions: science escape room, history escape room, literature escape room

name: Community Problem Identification and Proposal
description: Teams identify a real problem in their school or community (cafeteria waste, playground safety, homework overload, traffic congestion near school). They define the problem clearly, research its causes, generate multiple possible solutions, evaluate each against real constraints (cost, feasibility, stakeholder impact), and present a formal proposal. The process matters more than the polish — assessment focuses on problem definition, strategy use, collaboration quality, and reasoning.
learning_objectives: Identify and define a real-world problem clearly
Research causes and contributing factors collaboratively
Generate and evaluate multiple solutions against real constraints
Present a reasoned proposal to an audience and respond to questions
variations: Mini version (45 min): identify problem and brainstorm solutions without full research
Implementation: actually implement the winning proposal and evaluate results
Cross-team: two teams work on the same problem and compare their proposals

name: Systems Mapping
description: Learners take a seemingly simple issue (food waste in the school cafeteria, traffic congestion, student stress) and create a systems map showing all the factors that contribute to it and how they interact. They discover feedback loops, unintended connections, and leverage points where small changes might have large effects. Builds systems thinking — the ability to see problems as networks of interacting factors rather than isolated cause-and-effect chains.
learning_objectives: Identify multiple contributing factors to a complex problem
Map relationships and interactions between factors
Discover feedback loops and unintended connections
Identify leverage points where intervention might be most effective
variations: Digital version: use collaborative mapping software
Comparison: two groups map the same problem and compare their maps
Historical: map the factors that led to a historical event or decision

name: The Unintended Consequences Game
description: Learners propose a solution to a problem, then systematically explore what new problems the solution might create. Then they propose solutions to those new problems, and explore their consequences in turn. The chain continues for three rounds, building awareness that intervention in complex systems always has side effects and that good problem solvers anticipate these before implementing. Uses real-world examples where well-intentioned solutions created unexpected problems.
learning_objectives: Anticipate unintended consequences of proposed solutions
Trace second-order and third-order effects of interventions
Evaluate solutions not just for effectiveness but for side effects
Develop humility about the limits of simple solutions to complex problems
variations: Historical version: trace actual unintended consequences of real policies or inventions
Positive consequences: explore solutions whose side effects were unexpectedly beneficial
Debate format: one team proposes a solution, the other team identifies potential unintended consequences
